attributes.stem:
A 35-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with slowly deteriorating vision in her left eye over twelve months. She has no other symptoms. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. There is no history of trauma to her eyes.

On examination, she appears well. Her pulse rate is 74/min, blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 99% on room air, and temperature 37.5°C. Her visual acuity in the right eye is 6/6 and 6/20 in the left eye. When she did a free health check at her work two years ago, her visual acuity was 6/6 in both eyes. Both pupils are 4mm in diameter but the left pupil reacts sluggishly to light and there is a left relative afferent pupillary defect. Fundoscopy shows a pink right optic disc and a pale left optic disc. There is no disc cupping in either eye.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with a classic case of compressive optic neuropathy. The key features are slowly progressive unilateral vision loss over months, a relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD), and optic atrophy (pale disc) without disc cupping. These findings strongly suggest an extrinsic compression of the optic nerve.

Meningioma is the most likely diagnosis in this case. Optic nerve sheath meningiomas typically present with gradual, painless visual loss in one eye. They are more common in middle-aged women, matching this patient's demographic. The presence of optic atrophy (pale disc) without disc cupping is characteristic of compressive optic neuropathy. Meningiomas can arise from the optic nerve sheath itself or from adjacent structures like the anterior clinoid process, sphenoid wing, or tuberculum sellae, compressing the optic nerve.

Glaucoma typically presents with increased intraocular pressure and characteristic optic disc cupping, which is specifically noted to be absent in this patient. While primary open-angle glaucoma does cause gradual vision loss, it usually affects peripheral vision first and would be expected to show disc cupping on examination. The presence of a pale disc without cupping argues against glaucoma.

Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH) typically presents with bilateral papilledema, headache, and transient visual obscurations. This patient has unilateral optic atrophy rather than papilledema, and no mention of headache or other symptoms typical of IIH. While IIH is more common in young to middle-aged women, the unilateral nature of this patient's findings makes it an unlikely diagnosis.

Schwannoma of the orbit is rare and would typically cause proptosis, diplopia, and lid swelling due to the mass effect, none of which are described in this patient. Schwannomas arise from Schwann cells of peripheral nerves, and the optic nerve is a central nervous system tract without Schwann cells, making an optic nerve schwannoma anatomically implausible.

Multiple sclerosis (MS) typically causes optic neuritis, which presents with acute or subacute (days to weeks) painful vision loss, often with color desaturation. This patient's gradual progression over 12 months without pain is inconsistent with typical optic neuritis. Additionally, MS-related optic neuritis usually improves at least partially over time, whereas this patient has shown progressive deterioration with development of optic atrophy.
learningPoints: Optic nerve compression from a meningioma typically presents with slowly progressive unilateral vision loss and optic atrophy.
A relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD) and pale optic disc are key examination findings in optic nerve compression.
Meningiomas affecting the optic nerve are more common in middle-aged women.
references: Acheson J. Optic nerve and chiasmal disease. J Neurol 2000; 247:587-96. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/11041326/
Lee A, Bhat N, Bindiganavile S, Dugan C. Orbital Masses. In: Eye Wiki [online]. 2024. Available at: https://eyewiki.org/Orbital_Masses

Ogasawara C, Philbrick BD, Adamson DC. Meningioma: A Review of Epidemiology, Pathology, Diagnosis, Treatment, and Future Directions. Biomedicines. 2021 Mar 21;9(3):319.  Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC8004084/

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
